notes.anchor: you had fallen to me by lot as quaestor
notes.note: The quaestors were assigned to their provinces by lot (sortitio) each year; Cicero's warm opening signals genuine pleasure at the draw, and also a hint of anxiety, since Caldus had not yet arrived in Cilicia despite the province being active.

notes.anchor: the connection which the lot had assigned us
notes.note: The bond between a governor and his quaestor (necessitudo sortis) was treated in Roman tradition as quasi-filial and lifelong, comparable to the tie between a patron and client; Cicero invokes this convention to make clear how seriously he takes the appointment.

A Curius who was consobrinus (cousin on the maternal side) of Coelius Caldus and was on intimate terms with Cicero; not further identifiable from the surviving letters, but evidently a member of Cicero's social circle who had written a warm recommendation of his cousin.

notes.anchor: C. Vergilius, your kinsman and one of my closest friends
notes.note: C. Vergilius, likely the man criticised alongside Quintus Cicero in Cic. Q. fr. 1.2 as a former propraetor in that region; here Cicero stresses his closeness to Vergilius to underline the weight of the double recommendation Caldus was receiving.

notes.anchor: whatever marks of distinction shall set out from me
notes.note: The obelus in the Latin (†ad te proficiscentur) marks a textual crux; the sense is that Cicero will confer on Caldus whatever official honours and commendations lie within a governor's power to bestow, provided the quaestor arrives in the province in time.
